children.value:
So far we’ve worked with discrete distributions (PMF) and empirical CDFs.
But many real variables are conceptually continuous — birth weight, height, income.
For a continuous variable, the probability of any exact value is zero:

children.html: P(X=7.4321…)=0P(X = 7.4321\ldots) = 0P(X=7.4321…)=0

children.value: what is the probability of falling in a small interval?

children.html: P(a≤X≤b)=∫abf(x) dxP(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_a^b f(x)\, dxP(a≤X≤b)=∫ab​f(x)dx
f(x)f(x)f(x)

children.value: A histogram is an approximation of the PDF — but it depends on bin width.

is a smooth histogram that doesn’t require
you to choose bins. Instead, it places a smooth “kernel” (usually Gaussian) at
each data point and sums them:

children.html: f^(x)=1nh∑i=1nK ⁣(x−xih)\hat{f}(x) = \frac{1}{nh} \sum_{i=1}^n K\!\left(\frac{x - x_i}{h}\right)f^​(x)=nh1​i=1∑n​K(hx−xi​​)
hhh
KKK

children.value:
The bandwidth is the only free parameter. Too small → noisy, too large → oversmoothed.
Most libraries choose

children.html: hhh

children.value: automatically using Silverman’s rule of thumb:

children.html: h=1.06⋅σ^⋅n−1/5h = 1.06 \cdot \hat{\sigma} \cdot n^{-1/5}h=1.06⋅σ^⋅n−1/5

children.value: Now we have four ways to represent a distribution:

children.html: P(X=x)P(X = x)P(X=x)
XXX
P(X≤x)P(X \leq x)P(X≤x)

children.value: They all contain the same information — just organized differently.
is a summary statistic computed from powers of the data.

children.html: μ=E[X]=∫x f(x) dx\mu = E[X] = \int x\, f(x)\, dxμ=E[X]=∫xf(x)dx
σ2=E[(X−μ)2]=∫(x−μ)2f(x) dx\sigma^2 = E[(X - \mu)^2] = \int (x - \mu)^2 f(x)\, dxσ2=E[(X−μ)2]=∫(x−μ)2f(x)dx
skewness=E ⁣[(X−μσ)3]\text{skewness} = E\!\left[\left(\frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right)^3\right]skewness=E[(σX−μ​)3]

children.value: Positive skewness: long right tail (income, city sizes)

children.html: kurtosis=E ⁣[(X−μσ)4]−3\text{kurtosis} = E\!\left[\left(\frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right)^4\right] - 3kurtosis=E[(σX−μ​)4]−3

children.value: Positive kurtosis (leptokurtic): heavier tails than normal

— there are more very low-weight
babies (premature, small for gestational age) than very high-weight ones.

too — you can be very premature
(27 weeks) but not very post-term (almost never past 44 weeks).

— most mothers are in their 20s,
but the tail extends into the 40s.
Plot a KDE for birth weight. Try three different bandwidths. How does the shape change?
Compute skewness for birth weight, pregnancy length, and mother’s age.
Which NSFG variable has the most positive skewness? The most negative?
Plot the CDF and its derivative (approximate PDF) for birth weight on the same figure.
Implement a Gaussian KDE from scratch using the kernel formula above.
